faqs.a: Late February through early April is ideal for spring green-up. Starting early ensures your lawn has strong roots before summer heat hits and reduces stress during peak cooling season.
faqs.q: When should I start lawn care service in Mauldin?

faqs.a: Mauldin's hot, humid summers stress cool-season grasses. We recommend deep watering 1–2 times weekly, raise mowing height to 3–4 inches, and avoid heavy fertilization. Zoysia and Bermuda are better alternatives if renovation is viable.
faqs.q: How do I handle summer dormancy stress on my lawn?

faqs.a: Mauldin's clay compacts easily. We recommend aerating in spring and fall, adding organic compost to improve drainage, and soil testing to guide custom fertilization rather than guessing.
faqs.q: What's the best way to manage red clay soil in Mauldin?

faqs.a: Yes. We offer organic fertilization, integrated pest management, and targeted treatments only when needed—reducing chemicals while maintaining a healthy lawn.
faqs.q: Do you offer organic or chemical-free lawn care?

faqs.a: Standard plans run bi-weekly March–October, with adjustments for weather and growth rate. We customize frequency and timing to your lawn's unique needs and budget.
faqs.q: How often do you service yards, and can I customize my schedule?

brief.about_angle: Locally-rooted experts who understand Mauldin's red clay, summer heat, and seasonal pest patterns. Frame as neighborhood caretakers with deep roots, not transactional service provider. Highlight longevity, customer retention, and pride in the community.
differentiators: Local Mauldin-based expertise in SC Piedmont climate and soil conditions

Comprehensive bundled offerings (lawn care, basic landscaping, seasonal treatments as single contact)
brief.hero_subheadline: Professional lawn care built for Upstate South Carolina — from spring color to heat-stress resilience.
brief.tone_recommendation: Professional yet warm and neighborly. Emphasize local expertise and pride in serving Mauldin directly, not corporate efficiency. Use 'we' and 'your' language. Explain the *why* behind recommendations (soil science, climate adaptation) to build trust, but keep it accessible—avoid jargon.
